excerpt_text:
When he woke in the woods in the dark and the cold of the night he'd reach out to touch the child sleeping beside him. Nights dark beyond darkness and the days more gray each one than what had gone before. Like the onset of some cold glaucoma dimming away the world. His hand rose and fell softly with each precious breath. He pushed away the plastic tarpaulin and raised himself in the stinking robes and blankets and looked toward the east for any light but there was none. In the dream from which he'd wakened he had wandered in a cave where the child led him by the hand. Their light playing over the wet flowstone walls. Like pilgrims in a fable swallowed up and lost among the inward parts of some granitic beast. Deep stone flues where the water dripped and sang. Tolling in the silence the minutes of the earth and the hours and the days of it and the years without cease. Until they stood in a great stone room where lay a black and ancient lake. And on the far shore a creature that raised its dripping mouth from the rimstone pool and stared into the light with eyes dead white and sightless as the eggs of spiders. It swung its head low over the water as if to take the scent of what it could not see. Crouching there pale and naked and translucent, its alabaster bones cast up in shadow on the rocks behind it. Its bowels, its beating heart. The brain that pulsed in a dull glass bell. It swung its head from side to side and then gave out a low moan and turned and lurched away and loped soundlessly into the dark.

With the first gray light he rose and left the boy sleeping and walked out to the road and squatted and studied the country to the south. Barren, silent, godless. He thought the month was October but he wasnt sure. He hadnt kept a calendar for years. They were moving south. There'd be no surviving another winter here.
instruction:
Write a 350 word excerpt about the content below emulating the style and voice of Cormac McCarthy

Content: In this excerpt, the narrative is primarily in the third person, focusing on a man and a child in a post-apocalyptic setting. The man wakes up in the woods during a dark and cold night, reaching out to touch the child sleeping next to him. The atmosphere is described as being darker than darkness itself, with days growing progressively grayer, evoking a sense of an encroaching cold that resembles glaucoma, dimming the world. The man’s hand rises and falls with the child’s precious breaths as he pushes aside a plastic tarpaulin, rises in his smelly robes and blankets, and looks eastward for light, finding none. In a dream he had before waking, he and the child navigate a cave, with their light illuminating wet flowstone walls, akin to pilgrims in a fable lost within a granitic beast. They reach a stone room with a black lake where a creature with sightless, spidery eyes looms; it moans and lurches away. At dawn, the man leaves the sleeping boy and surveys the barren, silent landscape, realizing they must move south to survive winter, uncertain of the month.

excerpt_text:
When it was light enough to use the binoculars he glassed the valley below. Everything paling away into the murk. The soft ash blowing in loose swirls over the blacktop. He studied what he could see. The segments of road down there among the dead trees. Looking for anything of color. Any movement. Any trace of standing smoke. He lowered the glasses and pulled down the cotton mask from his face and wiped his nose on the back of his wrist and then glassed the country again. Then he just sat there holding the binoculars and watching the ashen daylight congeal over the land. He knew only that the child was his warrant. He said: If he is not the word of God God never spoke.

When he got back the boy was still asleep. He pulled the blue plastic tarp off of him and folded it and carried it out to the grocery cart and packed it and came back with their plates and some cornmeal cakes in a plastic bag and a plastic bottle of syrup. He spread the small tarp they used for a table on the ground and laid everything out and he took the pistol from his belt and laid it on the cloth and then he just sat watching the boy sleep. He'd pulled away his mask in the night and it was buried somewhere in the blankets. He watched the boy and he looked out through the trees toward the road. This was not a safe place. They could be seen from the road now it was day. The boy turned in the blankets. Then he opened his eyes. Hi, Papa, he said.

I'm right here.

I know.

An hour later they were on the road. He pushed the cart and both he and the boy carried knapsacks. In the knapsacks were essential things. In case they had to abandon the cart and make a run for it. Clamped to the handle of the cart was a chrome motorcycle mirror that he used to watch the road behind them. He shifted the pack higher on his shoulders and looked out over the wasted country. The road was empty. Below in the little valley the still gray serpentine of a river. Motionless and precise. Along the shore a burden of dead reeds. Are you okay? he said. The boy nodded. Then they set out along the blacktop in the gunmetal light, shuffling through the ash, each the other's world entire.
instruction:
Write a 416 word excerpt about the content below emulating the style and voice of Cormac McCarthy

Content: In the excerpt, the narrative follows a man who is observing a desolate landscape using binoculars. The setting is bleak, with ash blowing over a blackened road and a valley fading into murkiness. He is searching for any signs of life or color among the dead trees and road segments but finds none. The narration is in the third person. The man lowers the binoculars to wipe his nose, then resumes watching the ashen landscape. He reflects on his deep connection to a child, whom he considers his purpose or "warrant." He philosophically muses that if the child is not a manifestation of divinity, then divinity has never truly spoken.  Returning to his camp, he finds the boy still asleep, covered with a blue plastic tarp. The man removes the tarp, folds it, and tidies up by packing it into a grocery cart. He then retrieves plates, cornmeal cakes, and syrup, preparing a simple meal. A pistol, suggesting the need for protection, is set on a makeshift table. When the boy awakes, he greets his father with "Hi, Papa," reaffirming their bond. After packing up, they continue their journey on the road. The man, pushing a cart with essential items, vigilantly watches their back using a motorcycle mirror, showing his protective nature. Together, they traverse through an ash-laden landscape, interconnected as each other's whole world.

generations.generated_text: He raised the binoculars and looked. Ash blowing over the blackened road. In the binoculars the valley was just a gray trough losing itself in the murk. He moved the glasses back and forth. The segment of road was pristine. Except for the dust and the ash. Looking down he could see for miles. He could see the track of the road leading to the south and he thought about the boy. He moved the glasses along the tree line on the side of the valley. He searched the dead woods for signs of anything green. The gray and charcoal road winding off into the distance. Segments of it running through the trees and then disappearing again. He lowered the glasses and wiped his nose and then raised them again. He sat for a long time. The transit of a shadow of a high cloud across the land and the trees and the road. Then nothing. He lowered the glasses. It was the perfect day of his childhood. This the day to shape the days upon. He picked up the binoculars and scanned the valley plain again. Everything paling away into the murk. The soft ash blowing in loose swirls over the blacktop. He looked at the boy. He could see the disappointment in his face. I’m sorry it’s not blue, he said. That’s okay. He was looking at the dead gray waves tumbling in the breakers. You always said it would be blue, he said. What do you want to do? I dont know. The child was his warrant. He said: If he is not the word of God God never spoke. When he got back the boy was still asleep. He pulled the blue plastic tarp off of him and folded it and carried it out to the grocerycart and packed it and came back with their plates and some cornmeal cakes in a plastic bag and a plastic bottle of syrup. He spread the small tarp they used for a table on the ground and laid everything out and he took the pistol from his belt and laid it on the cloth and then he just sat watching the boy sleep. He’d pulled away his mask in the night and it was buried somewhere in the blankets. He watched the boy and he looked out through the trees toward the road. This was not a safe place. They could be seen from the road now it was day. The boy turned in his sleep and after a while he opened his eyes. Hi, Papa, he said. They ate and moved on, leaving the fire on the ground behind them. He pushed the cart and both he and the boy carried knapsacks. In the knapsacks were essential things in case they had to abandon the cart and make a run for it. Clamped to the handle of the cart was a chrome motorcycle mirror that he used to watch the road behind them. He shifted the pack on his shoulders and looked out over the wasted country. The road was empty. Below in the little valley the still gray serpentine of a river. Motionless and gray. He put his hand on the boy’s shoulder and turned him. He pointed. Look, he said. He looked. The boy looked. Then they went on and he pushed the cart and both he and the boy carried knapsacks. They were moving south. There’d be no surviving another winter here. They were the walking dead in a horror film. They slouched along and he continued to jab the blade of the shovel into the drifts of ash alongside the road and they went on. He looked at the boy. A word in a book. A chart and a compass. He thought the bloodcults must have all consumed one another. No one had seen the like of it upon the earth. They were moving south. There’d be no surviving another winter here. They were each the other’s world entire.

He lowered the binoculars and wiped his nose on his sleeve. Then he raised them again. The ash blew in a thin gray veil and in the valleylands the falls of snow had turned the woods to much the same color. The road descended through the still black trees. He raised the glasses higher. The pale sky. The sun a pale cold disc in the west. He lowered the glasses and sat watching the road below, the segments of it down there among the dead trees, looking for anything of color. Any movement. Any trace of standing smoke. No color. He lowered the glasses and pulled down the cotton mask from his face and wiped his nose again and then he just sat there, holding the binoculars, watching the road. He thought about his son. That the boy was all that stood between him and death. He is my warrant, he said. If he is not the word of God God never spoke. He went back to the camp and the boy was still asleep. He pulled the blue plastic tarp off of him and folded it carefully and carried it out to the grocerycart and packed it away. He came back with their plates and some cornmeal cakes in a plastic bag and a plastic bottle of syrup. He spread the small tarp they used for a table on the ground and laid everything out and he took the pistol from his belt and laid it on the cloth and then he just sat watching the boy sleep. He’d pulled away his mask in the night and it was buried somewhere in the blankets. He watched the boy and he looked out through the trees toward the road. This was not a safe place. They could be seen from the road now it was day. The boy turned in the blankets. Then he opened his eyes. Hi, Papa, he said. They ate the cakes and drank the last of the water and then they set out upon the road again. They plodded on, thin and filthy as street addicts. He pushed the cart and the boy walked beside him. An hour later they were on the road. He pushed the cart and both he and the boy carried knapsacks. In the knapsacks were essential things in case they had to abandon the cart and make a run for it. Clamped to the handle of the cart was a chrome motorcycle mirror that he used to watch the road behind them. He shifted the pack on his shoulders and looked back. Nothing. He looked at the sky. A single gray flake sifting down. He pushed the cart and the boy walked barefoot beside him, steering around the sharp rocks in the road. He looked at the boy. He could see the skeletal shape of his skull under the membrane of skin. Pale and thin. With eyes so dark they looked like holes in his face. He walked out in the gray light and stood and he saw for a brief moment the absolute truth of the world. The cold relentless circling of the intestate earth. Darkness implacable. The blind dogs of the sun in their running. The crushing black vacuum of the universe. And somewhere two hunted animals trembling like ground-foxes in their cover. Borrowed time and borrowed world and borrowed eyes with which to sorrow it. Each the other’s world entire.

excerpt_text:
The clocks stopped at 1:17. A long shear of light and then a series of low concussions. He got up and went to the window. What is it? she said. He didnt answer. He went into the bathroom and threw the lightswitch but the power was already gone. A dull rose glow in the window-glass. He dropped to one knee and raised the lever to stop the tub and then turned on both taps as far as they would go. She was standing in the doorway in her nightwear, clutching the jamb, cradling her belly in one hand. What is it? she said. What is happening?

I dont know.

Why are you taking a bath?

I'm not.

Once in those early years he'd wakened in a barren wood and lay listening to flocks of migratory birds overhead in that bitter dark. Their half muted crankings miles above where they circled the earth as senselessly as insects trooping the rim of a bowl. He wished them godspeed till they were gone. He never heard them again.

He'd a deck of cards he found in a bureau drawer in a house and the cards were worn and spindled and the two of clubs was missing but still they played sometimes by firelight wrapped in their blankets. He tried to remember the rules of childhood games. Old Maid. Some version of Whist. He was sure he had them mostly wrong and he made up new games and gave them made up names. Abnormal Fescue or Catbarf. Sometimes the child would ask him questions about the world that for him was not even a memory. He thought hard how to answer. There is no past. What would you like? But he stopped making things up because those things were not true either and the telling made him feel bad. The child had his own fantasies. How things would be in the south. Other children. He tried to keep a rein on this but his heart was not in it. Whose would be?

No lists of things to be done. The day providential to itself. The hour. There is no later. This is later. All things of grace and beauty such that one holds them to one's heart have a common provenance in pain. Their birth in grief and ashes. So, he whispered to the sleeping boy. I have you.
instruction:
Write a 395 word excerpt about the content below emulating the style and voice of Cormac McCarthy

Content: In this excerpt, the narrative is primarily in the third person, focusing on a man and a woman in the immediate aftermath of an unexplained catastrophe. The clocks have stopped at 1:17, and a burst of light followed by low rumbles suggest some kind of disaster. The man rises, checks the bathroom, and attempts to fill the bathtub with water before the supply is cut off. The woman, pregnant and in her nightwear, questions him anxiously. He provides no clear answer, only stating that he is not taking a bath, subtly indicating his intent to secure water. The scene transitions to a reflection on a time in the past when the man woke in a desolate forest, listening to unseen migratory birds. This memory highlights a theme of transience and loss. He also recalls finding a deck of cards and inventing games to play with a boy, with whom he seems to share a close bond. Despite trying to shield the boy from harsh truths, the man struggles with the futility of fabricating stories about the past. The passage concludes with a tender moment, as he silently acknowledges the profound connection he shares with the sleeping child, suggesting that love remains amidst their bleak reality.

generations.generated_text: The clocks stopped at 1:17. A long shear of light and then a series of low concussions. He got up and went to the window. What is it? she said. He didnt answer. He went into the bathroom and threw the lightswitch but the power was already gone. A dull rose glow in the windowglass. He dropped to one knee and raised the lever to stop the tub and then turned on both taps as far as they would go. She was standing in the doorway in her nightwear, clutching the jamb, cradling her belly in one hand. What is it? she said. What is it? I dont know. Why are you taking a bath? I'm not. He’d woken in a bleak wood in the dark and the cold of the gray dawn and heard the susurrus of migrating birds passing somewhere above in the darkness, their wings beating though he could not see them. He’d found a deck of cards half burned in some fire and he’d used them to make up names for himself. He often talked to himself. He said the names of things as if they were made only for this. He was learning to live alone. He’d found a deck of cards once and he’d carried them about for years in his coat. He taught the boy all the games he knew. When the wind was right and the smoke was not too bad they would sit up in the barn loft and play. They played for everything. What else was there? They were the walking dead in a horror film. They sat in the cave and watched the wind whip the grass and the trees. He’d tried to tell the boy about the world before. The boy had not been fooled. A lot of things you can say about the dead but it is not one of them that they are dumb. He was looking into the fire and he turned and looked at the boy. You said you wouldnt ever leave me. I know. But you might. My job is to take care of you. I was appointed to do that by God. I will kill anyone who touches you. Do you understand? Yes. He sat there cowled in the blanket. After a while he said: Are we going to die? Sometime. Not now. And we’re still going south. Yes. So we’ll be warm. Yes. Okay.

The clocks stopped at 1:17. A long shear of light and then a series of low concussions. He got up and went to the window. What is it? she said. He didnt answer. He went into the bathroom and threw the lightswitch but the power was already gone. A dull rose glow in the windowglass. He dropped to one knee and raised the lever to stop the tub and then turned on both taps as far as they would go. She was standing in the doorway in her nightwear, clutching the jamb, her eyes huge, her mouth loose. What is it? she said. What is it? I dont know. Why are you taking a bath? I’m not. Once in the dead of winter he had awakened in a house in the woods and come downstairs in the dark and stood in the cold kitchen waiting for he didnt know what. Then he’d taken up a candle and protected the flame and gone out to the road. It had snowed lightly in the night and the snow was frozen hard on the ground. He stood there for a long time. Finally he came back into the house and went upstairs. He got into bed beside the boy. He thought the boy was sleeping but then he said: What is it, Papa? What is it? In the nights in their thousands to come he’d often think of that child waking up in the sleep and devastation of the chilly woods. He’d found a deck of cards in a bureau drawer and he had taught the boy to play poker and he went out to the road and found a piece of chalk and marked out a rectangle on the stained concrete floor where they’d built their fire and he’d taught the boy chess on a board of sixtyfour squares he’d chalked on the floor. He tried to think of something to say but he could not. He’d had this problem before. He could not construct for the child’s pleasure the world he’d lost without constructing the loss as well and he thought perhaps the child had known this better than he. He looked at the boy and he looked at the claims of that proof sum. He thought the boy was sleeping but when he looked again the boy’s eyes were open. He knew only that the child was his warrant. He said: If he is not the word of God God never spoke.
